coredata.dc:title: Easy synthesis, characterization and cell cytotoxicity of green nano carbon dots using hydrothermal carbonization of Gum Tragacanth and chitosan bio-polymers for bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Moradi, Sajad
Sadrjavadi, Komail
Farhadian, Negin
Hosseinzadeh, Leila
Shahlaei, Mohsen
coredata.dc:description: Two environmentally friendly biopolymers, Gum Tragacanth and Chitosan, were used for one-step synthesis of photo-luminescent nano carbon dots using hydrothermal carbonization technique. In this study, free Gum Tragacanth and also in combination with chitosan are used as the carbon sources for preparing of nano carbon dots. The morphological aspects and chemical compositions of prepared carbon dots were characterized using various techniques such as Fourier transform infrared, dynamic light scattering, UV–visible, X-ray diffraction method, atomic force microscope and cyclic voltammetry. The Gum Tragacanth /chitosan and Gum Tragacanth based carbon dots have favorable luminescent properties and exhibited good photo-stability. As indicated by different spectroscopic and microscopic methods, the GT and GT/chitosan based carbon dots possesses excellent solubility as a result of many hydrophilic groups on the surface and good photo-stability. The obtained nano carbon dots include a large number of surface functionalized groups. Their excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity has been confirmed using MTT assay. Also, the cellular uptake studies show that the Gum Tragacanth/chitosan based carbon dots were directly internalized into the cells more than other nano carbon dot. These results indicated that owing to the excellent features of the generated carbon dots based on bio-polymer, they can be used, as ideal non-toxic markers, in bio-sensing, biological labeling, medical diagnostics, optoelectronic devices and bio-imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Nano carbon dots
Gum Tragacanth
Hydrothermal method
Photo-luminescent

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of the bio-polymer based Carbon dots: A) Gum Tragacanth, B) Gum Tragacanth – chitosan.
The photo stability of the bio-polymer based carbon dots during different days.
Viscometer analysis of carbon dot solution during the days of the test.
AFM images for A) GT/chitosan based carbon dots; B) GT based carbon dots.
TEM imaging of as prepared carbon dot A) GT/chitosan based carbon dots; B) GT based carbon dots.
FT-IR spectrum of the bio-polymer based carbon dots.
EDX analysis of obtained dots A) GT/chitosan based carbon dots; B) GT based carbon dots.
XRD patterns of as-prepared carbon dots in comparison with those of graphite.
UV–vis spectroscopy of the bio-polymer based carbon dots.
Cyclic voltammograms of bio-polymer based carbon dots.
Effects of GT/chitosan (A) and GT (B) based carbon dots on the HUVEC cells viability.

Fluorescence microscope images demonstrating the intracellular GT/chitosan and GT based carbon dots distribution in HUVEC cell line. (A) Control cells incubated without compound. (B) Cells exposed to 50μg/ml of GT/chitosan and (C) GT based carbon dots for 4h.

head.ce:title: Easy synthesis, characterization and cell cytotoxicity of green nano carbon dots using hydrothermal carbonization of Gum Tragacanth and chitosan bio-polymers for bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano Drug Delivery Research Center, School of Pharmacy, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceuticas Sciences Research Center, School of Pharmacy, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Medical Biology Research Center, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Medicinal Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, 67346-67149 Kermanshah, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two environmentally friendly biopolymers, Gum Tragacanth and Chitosan, were used for one-step synthesis of photo-luminescent nano carbon dots using hydrothermal carbonization technique. In this study, free Gum Tragacanth and also in combination with chitosan are used as the carbon sources for preparing of nano carbon dots. The morphological aspects and chemical compositions of prepared carbon dots were characterized using various techniques such as Fourier transform infrared, dynamic light scattering, UV–visible, X-ray diffraction method, atomic force microscope and cyclic voltammetry. The Gum Tragacanth /chitosan and Gum Tragacanth based carbon dots have favorable luminescent properties and exhibited good photo-stability. As indicated by different spectroscopic and microscopic methods, the GT and GT/chitosan based carbon dots possesses excellent solubility as a result of many hydrophilic groups on the surface and good photo-stability. The obtained nano carbon dots include a large number of surface functionalized groups. Their excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity has been confirmed using MTT assay. Also, the cellular uptake studies show that the Gum Tragacanth/chitosan based carbon dots were directly internalized into the cells more than other nano carbon dot. These results indicated that owing to the excellent features of the generated carbon dots based on bio-polymer, they can be used, as ideal non-toxic markers, in bio-sensing, biological labeling, medical diagnostics, optoelectronic devices and bio-imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Generally organic dyes and semi-conductor quantum dots (QDs) provide excellent luminescence performance which is mainly imperative for in vivo and in vitro optical imaging purposes [1]. The great problem that limits the use of these materials in biological applications is the toxicity of components which are harmful to the environment and human body [2]. Accordingly, a new category of benign carbon based photo-luminescent nanostructures called carbon dots (CDs), have been developed by several researchers [3–6]. Carbon dots are attracted a lot of attention due to their unique characteristics such as facile synthesis, high aqueous solubility, good biological compatibility, low environmental toxicity, strong and tunable photo-luminescence, easy functionalization, lack of blinking, low photo-bleaching and robust chemical inertness [1,7–9]. Carbon dots have wide potential for application in various fields and they are applicable in bio-imaging [10,11], biosensors [12], optoelectronic devices [13], metal cations analysis [9,14,15], medical diagnosis [16] and catalysis [17]. Carbon dots can be prepared using etching with larger carbon materials for example laser ablation, hydrothermal treatment [18], plasma treatment [19] electrochemical oxidation, hot injection, microwave irradiation and pyrolysis [20–22]. Hydrothermal carbonization is a most widely used method for the preparation of carbon dots. This simple method doesn't require high temperature and strongly toxic chemicals and also can be used for exploring the usefulness of carbon resources [9,23]. Recently various renewable resources such as chitosan gel [24], amino acids [25], watermelon peel [26], pomelo peel [27], gelatin [28], coffee grounds [29], soy milk [30] and low-cost organic chemicals [31,32] were applied as precursors to synthesis carbon dots. The objective of the present study is to explore carbohydrate based material Gum Tragacanth (GT) as natural bio-degradable and safe precursor for synthesis of bio-friendly luminescent carbon dots. This biopolymer has carboxylic groups on its surface that helps researchers to covalently functionalize it for targeting operations. Moreover, due to the extensive application of carbon dots in many areas, they could enter the environment from various routes. Hence, investigation of their synthesis, characterizations, toxicity seems necessary. Ke et al. [33] and Han et al. [34] reported that nitrogen containing carbon dots possesses better photo-luminescence properties than other different types of carbon dots. This enhancement in the photo-luminescence efficiency is due to that nitrogen has a comparable atomic size to carbon and also it has five valence electrons to bind with carbon atoms. Chitosan was used because it contains nitrogen atom and hence can improve the photo-luminescence characteristics of the carbon dots. After green synthesize of carbon dots using carbonization of GT and GT/chitosan biopolymers, the surface functional groups, size, morphology and cytotoxicity of them were determined by using various experimental techniques.
Gum Tragacanth was prepared as reported in our previous study [35] and Chitosan were obtained from Sigma Aldrich.

Luminescent carbon dots were synthetized using hydrothermal carbonization of the two mentioned biopolymers. GT solution (1% w/w) was prepared by dissolving GT powder in deionized water, kept at room temperature overnight. Similarly, the chitosan solution in acetic acid (1% v/v) was obtained. Two different CDs were construct; Once the pure GT solution and at the next time, an equal amounts of GT and chitosan solutions were used for the carbon dot preparation. Each of these solutions was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and for a period of 45min heated at 250°C. After filtration, to eliminate the unwanted organic substances the solution was washed with dichloromethane (50% v/v) under gentle shaking for 15min followed by centrifugation at 8000rpm for 15min. Finally the supernatant was collected and freeze dried for future use.

FT-IR experiments were performed at room temperature on a Shimadzu IR2 FT-IR spectrometer (Japan). Elemental Analysis of Carbon Dots was done using an EDX method. To study the spectral properties of synthesized carbon dots, the ultraviolet (UV) absorption spectra were obtained using a spectrophotometer Agilent 8453 (USA) from 200 to 700nm. Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a PerkinElmer LS 45 (USA) Fluorescence spectrometer. Crystallinity of the carbon dot samples was investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns with a Inel Equaniox 3000 (France), 30kV, 20mA, K alpha Cu: 1.54Å. The sizes and zeta potentials of the carbon dot particles were determined with a Malvern zetasizer instrument (UK). To confirm the determined size values which obtained by DLS, AFM measurements were done through an AFM Explorer microscope. Exact size of the nanoparticles was finally evaluated using TEM analysis. The reduction and oxidation potentials of the GT and GT/chitosan based carbon dots were measured using cyclic voltammetry (CV) by a Potentiostat Metrohm μlab III (Switzerland). For electrochemical experiments a standard three-electrode system including a Ag/AgCl/3M KCl, a platinum wire and a Glassy carbon electrode as an reference, counter and working electrode was applied. Cyclic voltammograms (scan rate of 100mVs−1 within a potential scan range of −0.5 to 0.5V) were measured in 0.01M of PBS solution (pH of 7.4).

The cytotoxic impacts of GT and GT/chitosan based carbon dots towards human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC cell line) which obtained from Pasteur Institute of Iran (Tehran, Iran) was carried out through conventional MTT assay. The Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM-F12) supplemented with fetal bovine serum (10% v/v) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin (100U/ml:100U/ml) was used for cell grows at 37°C in a humidified incubator containing 5% CO2. The cells were plated in 96-well cell culture plates at a cell density of 15×104 cells per well. After 24h the spent medium was replaced with fresh medium containing increasing concentrations (0–50μg/ml) of the carbon dots. A negative control of untreated cells was included. The cells were incubated for a further 24h, after which a 0.5mg/ml MTT solution (10μl per well) was added and the cells incubated for an additional 4h. Then, the medium was removed, and the purple Formosan crystals were dissolved in DMSO (200μl/well). Absorbance was determined on an ELISA plate reader (Biotek, H1 M) with a test wave length of 570nm and a reference wavelength of 630nm to obtain sample signal (OD570–OD630). For evaluation cellular uptake HUVEC cells were plated in 6-well cell culture plates at a cell density of 7×105 cells per well. After overnight cells were treated with as prepared carbon dots at a concentration of 50μg/ml and then the plates transferred to a 37°C incubator for 4h. After that fluorescence images were taken at 100 magnifications under a fluorescence microscope (Model: Micros AUSTR1A) with imaging system. For cells without any treatment were taken as a staining control a concentration of 50μg/ml were added to medium.

Fig. 1 represents the photo-luminescence spectra of the prepared carbon dots excited at wide range of wavelengths. As shown in this figure, the excitation/emission spectra of the carbon dots produced from GT alone (Fig. 1A), indicate that spectrum profile is a little different in comparison with that of the carbon dots prepared using equal volume ratio of both GT and chitosan solutions (Fig. 1B). It was observed that the aqueous GT-carbon dots solution gave excitation at 340nm followed by emission peak at 428nm. Carbon dots prepared from chitosan/GT solutions exhibits emission band at 413nm with 317nm excitation wavelength.

To determine the presence of the functional groups on the bio-polymer based carbon dots, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was used. For the carbon dots obtained using GT and GT/chitosan, the characteristic absorption peaks around 3400, 2930, 1290 and 1060cm−1 are correspond to symmetric stretching of OH, CH and etheric COC and asymmetric stretching of carbohydrate skeletal of both GT and GT-chitosan dots. Other the peaks emerge at, 1743 and 1414cm−1 are attributed to stretching of CO in carbonyl groups and asymmetric stretching of CO2 of carboxylic acid in both nano dots (Fig. 6 ). These results explain the presence of the both carboxylic and hydroxyl groups originating from carbohydrates in bio-polymers. Probably as a result of these functional groups, the synthesized carbon dots show good water solubility [37].

Also peaks available at 1570 and 1410cm−1 are based on the published study by Lawrie et al. is related to amide and amine groups. Those researchers using the complementary analysis of XPS, showed that chitosan in combination with alginate, has two strong peaks in these regions, which is related to reduction states of 1s and 3s for nitrogen [38]. Since Gum Tragacanth has very similarity to alginate with respect to its sugar structure and also acidic and alcoholic groups, the strength of the peaks in regions mentioned above, in addition to acidic groups, can be attributed to the different protonation states of chitosan.

In order to determine the amount of different elements in carbon dots, and in particular the determination of the amount of nitrogen in their structure, the analysis of the EDX is performed and the results are shown in (Fig. 7 ). These results confirm the presence of nitrogen in the final structure of the chitosan-containing nanoparticles, as well as the dispersion of it is completely uniform.

According to the Debye Scherer equation [39], more broadness in XRD peaks resulted in smaller crystallite size. From XRD pattern of GT based carbon dots in comparison to that of pure graphite, a broader peak observed at 2θ=22° indicate a smaller crystalline size which could be assigned to disturbances of non-repeatable side chains in GT structure (Fig. 8 ). But in the case of the XRD patterns for the GT/chitosan based carbon dots, two peaks at 2θ=27° & 31°, are observed which both are sharper than that of GT. It seems that because of the more homogeneity and repeatability of the chitosan structure, the crystallite grows and thereby its size is bigger than that of GT based carbon dots. Additionally, chitosan has helped GT to make bigger crystallite as in the GT/chitosan XRD pattern the broad peak related to GT didn't observed. In the other hand according to brag equation smaller distance of crystal planes appeared in bigger 2θ. As can be seen in Fig. 8 the peaks related to GT/chitosan are in the right of those of GT base Nano carbon dots that can be because of more compact structure in GT/chitosan dots.

In summary, GT and GT/chitosan solutions were utilized to preparation of the photo-luminescent carbon dots via hydrothermal method. As indicated by different spectroscopic and microscopic methods, the GT and GT/chitosan based carbon dots possesses excellent solubility as a result of many hydrophilic groups on the surface and good photo-stability. The graphitic nature of the carbon was identified by X-ray diffraction. Low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility of the as-prepared carbon dots were confirmed using MTT and cellular uptake assays. Finally, owing to the excellent features of the bio-polymer based carbon dots, they can be used in bio-sensing, biological labeling, medical diagnostics, optoelectronic devices and bio-imaging.
